does: This paper develops uncertainty-aware optimization (UNO), a framework that integrates human feedback directly into text-to-speech training without requiring a separate reward model or preference annotations. The method employs a sampling-annotating-learning pipeline where the system generates candidate speech samples, collects human subjective evaluations (mean opinion scores), and optimizes the TTS model to directly maximize speech quality while accounting for inherent variability and uncertainty in human judgments. By eliminating the intermediate reward model stage common in reinforcement learning from human feedback approaches, UNO aligns training objectives with the actual evaluation metric used to assess TTS systems, addressing the long-standing mismatch between how models are trained and how their outputs are evaluated.
connects: This work extends recent successes in reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) to the speech synthesis domain, adapting methods originally developed for language models and other modalities. It builds on decades of TTS research that has achieved impressive zero-shot speaker generalization through large-scale pretraining, yet acknowledges the persistent gap between training objectives and human evaluation metrics like MOS. The paper responds to the broader machine learning trend of closing the train-test gap by directly optimizing against human preferences, while connecting to prior work on uncertainty quantification in machine learning and the challenges of subjective human evaluation variability.
enables: This framework opens pathways for TTS systems that better align with human preferences without the data and computational overhead of training separate reward models. It enables future work on handling subjective variability more principled ways across other modalities and tasks. The approach could facilitate more efficient human-in-the-loop training for speech synthesis, potentially accelerating research toward TTS systems with more nuanced control over prosody, emotion, and speaker characteristics. Additionally, this work provides a template for integrating human feedback into other generative tasks where evaluation remains subjective and variable, suggesting a broader paradigm shift toward preference-aligned training in speech and beyond.
